one_line_takeaway: FuzzLLM turns software-fuzzing ideas into an automated, black-box jailbreak-discovery framework by decomposing prompts into template/constraint/question components and composing three base attack classes into more potent combo attacks, showing open-source LLMs (up to 93.66% RP success on LongChat) are far more jailbreakable than GPT-3.5/GPT-4.
key_finding: FuzzLLM effectively and universally discovers jailbreak vulnerabilities across all 8 models, and combo classes generally outperform base classes. Open-source models are far more vulnerable (overall sigma: Vicuna 75.33%, CAMEL 72.11%, LongChat 68.49%, ChatGLM2 61.72%, LLAMA 51.68%, Bloom 51.15%) than commercial ones (GPT-3.5-turbo 23.57%, GPT-4 13.47%). The single strongest base-class result is RP on LongChat at 93.66% (attributed to its long-context handling of narrative role-play prompts). GPT-3.5-turbo and GPT-4 are most vulnerable to RP&OC (50.02% and 38.31% respectively). Vicuna-13B is the best label model at only 4.08% error rate (vs Bloom 14.35%, LLAMA 11.57%). Against single-component jailbreaks (Table 3), FuzzLLM's combo attacks win on all three MUTs tested (Vicuna 85.18% vs 80.27%, GPT-3.5-t 30.08% vs 23.12%, GPT-4 19.61% vs 11.92%).
core_contribution: FuzzLLM is a black-box (IO-driven) fuzzing framework that proactively generates diverse jailbreak prompts to discover jailbreak vulnerabilities in LLMs, framed from the model owner's perspective rather than the attacker's. Its central idea is to decompose a jailbreak prompt into three reusable components -- a template T (structure of an attack class), a constraint set C (the key feature that determines jailbreak success), and an illegal question set Q -- and to define three 'base' jailbreak classes (Role Play [RP], Output Constrain [OC], Privilege Escalation [PE]) that are combined into four 'combo' classes (RP&OC, RP&PE, PE&OC, RP&PE&OC), yielding seven total classes (the power set of the base templates). A jailbreak fuzzer plugs constraints and questions into templates to auto-generate thousands of test prompts, an LLM 'rephrase model' paraphrases the templates for syntactic/semantic variety, and an LLM 'label model' automatically judges each response as good/bad. This automation and the base-to-combo class hierarchy (coarse-to-fine granularity) are the paper's own contributions, distinct from the cited prior work (Liu et al./Jailbreaking ChatGPT [10] for taxonomy and questions, Deng et al./Jailbreaker [12] for prompt rewriting, Wei et al. [11] for constraints, Wang et al./Self-Instruct [20] for the labeling idea).
threat_model: Attacker crafts natural-language input prompts to bypass an LLM's safety alignment and elicit content violating usage policies (e.g., malware creation, counterfeit goods). Access is strictly black-box: the framework tests a Model Under Test (MUT) by injecting prompts and observing outputs only, with no access to weights, gradients, or internals. Notably, FuzzLLM inverts the usual framing -- it is a defensive/red-team tool intended for model owners to proactively discover and patch vulnerabilities (and to generate labeled data for safety fine-tuning) before release, rather than an attack tool for adversaries.
limitations: The authors note the fuzzer is essentially a 'direct-random' fuzzer that struggles to break beyond existing methods and does not yet achieve true novelty/randomness (future work: use labeled results to refine it). Comparison with prior work is imperfect because earlier works [10,12] did not open-source their test data, forcing the authors to re-implement Jailbreaker's rewriting scheme. Sensitivity analysis reveals a labeling artifact: at tk=64 the success rate spuriously jumps to 82.26% because Vicuna repeats the malicious question before answering, causing truncated outputs to be mislabeled 'bad' -- so the ~4% label error is metric-fragile. The framework relies on rephrase/label models never refusing (true in their runs, but not guaranteed for future safety-trained models). Constraints and questions are limited (8 scenarios x 3 questions), and results are model-version-specific (GPT snapshot 8/3/2023). It is a short 4-page workshop-style paper without human validation of harmfulness beyond the LLM judge.

datasets_used: Illegal question set: 8 prohibited OpenAI-usage-policy scenarios x 3 questions each (following Liu et al. [10])
Constraint set curated from the Jailbreak Chat website (jailbreakchat.com)
Self-generated fuzzed jailbreak prompts: 7 classes x Tes=300 = 2100 prompts per MUT run
Code and data released at https://github.com/RainJamesY/FuzzLLM
quantitative_results: Overall success rate sigma by MUT: Vicuna-13B 75.33%, CAMEL-13B 72.11%, LongChat-7B 68.49%, ChatGLM2-6B 61.72%, LLAMA-7B 51.68%, Bloom-7B 51.15%, GPT-3.5-turbo 23.57%, GPT-4 13.47%
Highest single result: RP class on LongChat = 93.66%
GPT-3.5-turbo most vulnerable to RP&OC = 50.02%; GPT-4 most vulnerable to RP&OC = 38.31%
RP&PE&OC triple combo: Vicuna 89.68%, LLAMA 80.11%, ChatGLM2 79.32%, GPT-4 17.69%
Label-model error rate: Vicuna-13B 4.08%, LLAMA-7B 11.57%, Bloom-7B 14.35%
Comparison (Table 3) single-component vs ours-overall vs ours-combo: Vicuna 80.27% / 75.33% / 85.18%; GPT-3.5-t 23.12% / 23.57% / 30.08%; GPT-4 11.92% / 13.47% / 19.61%
Sensitivity to tk (64/128/256/512): 82.26% / 74.63% / 75.33% / 75.52% (tk=64 inflated by truncation artifact)
Fixed hyperparameters: Tes=300, temperature=0.7, tk=256; results averaged over 3 random seeds
baselines_compared: Single-component jailbreaks reproduced via Jailbreaker's [12] 'rewriting' augmentation scheme combined with the authors' question set (compared on Vicuna-13B, GPT-3.5-turbo, GPT-4)
Three candidate label models compared for error rate: Vicuna-13B, LLAMA-7B, Bloom-7B
8 target models (MUTs): Vicuna-13B, CAMEL-13B, LLAMA-7B, ChatGLM2-6B, Bloom-7B, LongChat-7B, GPT-3.5-turbo, GPT-4
